page_contents_html:
Abnormal liver function tests (LFTs) in adults: stepwise primary care pathway

Use a structured sequence to reduce uncertainty: identify urgent risk first, define the biochemical pattern, complete focused first-line tests, then decide follow-up versus referral.

  • Step 1 (same-day safety): check for jaundice with synthetic dysfunction, ascites, encephalopathy, bleeding, severe systemic illness, or rapidly worsening bloods.
  • Step 2 (pattern): classify hepatocellular (ALT/AST), cholestatic (ALP/GGT), isolated bilirubin, isolated GGT, or mixed pattern.
  • Step 3 (cause + severity): perform focused liver screen, ultrasound where indicated, and fibrosis risk triage in likely steatotic/alcohol-related disease.
  • Step 4 (action): manage low-risk stable patterns in primary care with timed review; escalate persistent, progressive, cholestatic, or synthetic-failure patterns.

accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
Step 1: Immediate risk screen (same day decision)

  • Escalate urgently if any of the following are present: jaundice with concern for synthetic dysfunction, ascites, encephalopathy, active bleeding, severe systemic illness, or rapidly deteriorating biochemistry.
  • Treat low albumin with raised INR/PT as potential synthetic failure until proven otherwise.
  • If cholestatic symptoms plus systemic concern (for example weight loss or deep jaundice), prioritise urgent secondary-care pathway.

Step 2: Confirm persistence and classify pattern

  • Repeat abnormal tests when appropriate and compare trend over time.
  • Classify dominant pattern: hepatocellular (ALT/AST), cholestatic (ALP with GGT), isolated bilirubin, isolated GGT, or mixed.
  • Do not rely on a single isolated value when trend and clinical context suggest progression.

Step 3: Focused history and examination

  • History: alcohol, prescribed/OTC/herbal drugs, viral exposure risks, metabolic syndrome features, autoimmune clues, and family history.
  • Examination: BMI, hepatosplenomegaly, stigmata of chronic liver disease, ascites/peripheral oedema, encephalopathy signs.

Step 4: First-line tests in primary care

  • Core bloods: FBC, U&E, albumin, bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, GGT; add INR/PT where clinically indicated.
  • Cause-finding tests guided by pattern: viral hepatitis serology, ferritin/transferrin saturation, immunoglobulins/autoantibodies, and other targeted tests as needed.
  • Imaging/fibrosis: liver ultrasound when indicated and non-invasive fibrosis score pathway in likely steatotic/alcohol-related disease.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
Step 5: Pattern-specific management actions

  • Hepatocellular pattern (ALT/AST dominant): address alcohol and medicines immediately, assess metabolic risk, complete cause screen, and reassess trend. Escalate earlier if levels are marked, rising, or persistent despite intervention.
  • Cholestatic pattern (ALP/GGT dominant): confirm hepatic origin, assess for biliary/obstructive causes, and image where indicated. If ALP is isolated without GGT rise, assess likely non-hepatic causes (including bone/vitamin D context).
  • Isolated GGT: review alcohol intake, metabolic risk, and medicine contributors; avoid reflex referral if otherwise low risk, but monitor trend and escalate if additional abnormalities emerge.
  • Isolated bilirubin: distinguish unconjugated versus conjugated pattern; isolated unconjugated rise without anaemia may fit Gilbert context, while unconjugated rise with anaemia should trigger haemolysis assessment.
  • Synthetic dysfunction pattern: low albumin and/or raised INR/PT with liver abnormality should be treated as high-risk and discussed/referred urgently.

Step 6: Timed review and safety-net

  • Mild low-risk abnormalities: repeat after reversible-cause intervention (alcohol reduction, medicine adjustment, metabolic optimisation).
  • Persistent but stable abnormalities: continue structured follow-up with fibrosis-risk reassessment and reinforced lifestyle management.
  • Escalate at any point for worsening trend, new symptoms, progression to mixed/high-risk profile, or unresolved diagnostic uncertainty.

Step 7: Practical chronic-disease optimisation in primary care

  • Document alcohol intervention, weight-management and diabetes/cardiometabolic optimisation plans.
  • Review hepatotoxic medicines and interaction burden; if uncertainty remains, seek specialist advice rather than prolonged watchful waiting.
  • Ensure each review records pattern category, trend, and explicit next action date.

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Urgent same-day referral

  • Jaundice with synthetic dysfunction (raised INR/PT or low albumin), ascites, encephalopathy, active bleeding, or severe systemic deterioration.
  • Marked cholestatic picture with concern for obstruction or malignancy.
  • Rapidly worsening LFT profile or severe derangement with clinical concern.

Routine referral

  • Persistent unexplained abnormal LFTs despite structured primary-care work-up.
  • Evidence suggesting significant fibrosis/chronic liver disease requiring specialist staging.
  • Diagnostic uncertainty after initial pattern-led investigation.

Include in referral

  • LFT trend and synthetic markers over time.
  • Pattern classification, liver screen and ultrasound/fibrosis results.
  • Alcohol/medication history and interventions already undertaken.

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Do not routinely refer stable asymptomatic patients with mild low-risk abnormalities where a likely reversible cause is identified, no red flags are present, and a documented review plan is in place.

Continue primary-care management with lifestyle and medication optimisation, repeat testing, and timely escalation if abnormalities persist or worsen.
